title: Blog - Custom MDX site
subtitle: A dedicated writing system with full control over structure, components, and publishing rhythm

sections.title: Why split writing from the portfolio?
paragraphs: The original portfolio had a blog section, but it was too tightly coupled to the site around it. Writing needed its own system: separate information architecture, stronger MDX primitives, and enough control to support essays, technical notes, and teaching material without reshaping the portfolio every time.
The dedicated blog became a small publishing product rather than a page on a personal site.

pullQuote.text: The blog itself is the artifact: a system for writing, teaching, and shaping technical context without fighting the container.

sections.title: The questions we had to answer

decisions.title: Why use MDX instead of a headless CMS?
decisions.teaser: The content needed custom rendering and version control more than an editor UI.
decisions.answer: MDX keeps content close to the components that explain it. Posts can include custom callouts, diagrams, code blocks, and structured sections while still living in git with the rest of the project.
alternatives: Headless CMS with rich text fields
Plain Markdown with limited custom components

decisions.title: How much design should a technical blog have?
decisions.teaser: Too little structure makes posts hard to scan; too much visual design distracts from the writing.
decisions.answer: The design direction is restrained: readable type, strong spacing, careful code treatment, and reusable components for recurring teaching patterns. The interface should support attention rather than perform as a portfolio piece.

decisions.title: What should stay separate from the portfolio?
decisions.teaser: The blog has different navigation and content needs than the main site.
decisions.answer: The blog is deployed separately so its routes, content model, and design system can evolve independently. The portfolio can reference the blog without inheriting its complexity.
alternatives: Keep posts under the portfolio route tree
Duplicate styles and components between both sites

callout.text: A writing system is infrastructure. The better the primitives are, the less friction there is between having an idea and publishing it well.

designDecision.title: Component-Driven MDX
designDecision.description: Posts use MDX components for recurring structures like notes, examples, and code explanations.
designDecision.why: Reusable components keep posts consistent without forcing every article into the same layout.

constraints.title: Writing needs low friction
constraints.description: If publishing requires too much ceremony, the system will not get used.

constraints.title: Code examples need care
constraints.description: Technical writing depends on code blocks, inline explanations, and examples that stay readable on mobile.

constraints.title: The blog should evolve independently
constraints.description: The portfolio and writing site serve different purposes and should not block each other's changes.

sections.title: What I chose not to optimize yet

tradeoffs.title: No CMS editor
tradeoffs.whatItCost: No browser-based editing or non-technical authoring flow
tradeoffs.whenWeReconsider: If the site needs guest authors or editorial collaboration

tradeoffs.title: No complex taxonomy
tradeoffs.whenWeReconsider: Once there is enough writing volume to justify stronger categorization

reflections.insight: A blog is easier to maintain when it has product boundaries. Separating it from the portfolio made both systems simpler.
reflections.transferable: Split systems when their publishing cadence, information architecture, and user goals differ.

reflections.insight: Good writing tools are quiet. The best components disappear while drafting and only show their value when the post is read.
reflections.transferable: Build authoring primitives around repeated real needs, not decorative possibilities.

sections.title: The work ahead
sections.intro: The site is live, but the writing system can still get sharper:

items.title: Improve post templates
items.description: Add stronger defaults for essays, technical walkthroughs, and short notes.

items.title: Refine discovery
items.description: Introduce lightweight topic pages once there are enough posts to make them useful.

items.title: Keep examples reusable
items.description: Turn repeated explanation patterns into MDX components only after they prove up across multiple posts.

problem: Technical writing needed its own publishing system instead of being tightly coupled to the portfolio.
solution: A dedicated MDX-based site with custom content primitives, readable defaults, and independent deployment.
role: Information architecture, MDX component design, and publishing workflow.
proof: The writing system separates content structure, custom components, and deployment cadence from the main portfolio.
challenges: Keeping authoring friction low while supporting rich posts
Designing reusable MDX components without overfitting early content
Separating the blog from the portfolio without duplicating every primitive
learnings: Publishing systems need product boundaries as much as visual polish.
Good writing tools should make the common path quiet and predictable.
